course_info.title: Interaction Design
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 1 hour per session

learning_objectives: Apply the five dimensions of interaction design: words, visuals, objects, time, behavior
Design interactions that feel natural and responsive
Evaluate existing interactions against established IxD principles
readings: Designing Interactions by Bill Moggridge, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Interaction design governs the conversation between users and products. Every tap, swipe, hover, and keystroke is part of a dialogue that must feel natural, responsive, and meaningful.
key_concepts: Five dimensions of IxD: words, visual representations, physical objects/space, time, behavior
Feedback loops: every action must have a visible, immediate response
Direct manipulation: objects respond as if physically grabbed
Consistency patterns: internal consistency vs. platform conventions
The 100ms, 1s, 10s response time thresholds
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deconstructing the interaction design of iOS pull-to-refresh — analyzing the trigger, rules, feedback, and loops that make it feel satisfying
setup_requirements: Mobile device for testing gesture interactions

review.value: Words (labels, prompts), Visuals (icons, images), Objects (physical hardware context), Time (animation, duration), Behavior (how the product responds to user actions)
100ms = feels instant, 1 second = noticeable delay (show activity indicator), 10 seconds = attention lost (show progress bar with percentage)

learning_objectives: Decompose microinteractions into their four structural components
Design microinteractions that communicate system state clearly
Use animation purposefully to provide feedback and guide attention

lecture_notes.introduction: Microinteractions are the contained product moments that accomplish a single task — toggling a setting, liking a post, setting an alarm. They are where interaction design becomes tangible and where delight lives.
key_concepts: Trigger: what initiates the interaction (user action or system event)
Rules: what happens when triggered (the logic)
Feedback: how the system communicates what happened (visual, audio, haptic)
Loops and modes: what happens over repeated use or in different states
Signature moments: microinteractions that define product personality
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing the complete microinteraction for a 'favorite' button — from trigger through animation, sound, and repeat behavior — in Figma with Smart Animate

review.value: Trigger (user tap) > Rules (add to favorites, update count) > Feedback (heart fills red, count increments, subtle bounce animation) > Loop (tapping again unfavorites with reverse animation)
Microinteractions so well-designed they become part of the product's identity — Facebook's reaction animations, Slack's message sending animation, Apple's unlock haptic

learning_objectives: Apply the 12 principles of animation to UI motion design
Choose appropriate easing curves for different interaction types
Design transitions that maintain spatial and temporal continuity
readings: Animation at Work by Rachel Nabors, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Motion in UI is not decoration — it communicates relationships, provides feedback, and guides attention. Every animation must have a purpose: what information does this motion convey?
key_concepts: The 12 principles of animation adapted for UI: ease-in/out, anticipation, follow-through
Easing curves: linear, ease-in, ease-out, ease-in-out, spring physics
Duration guidelines: 100-300ms for micro, 300-500ms for macro transitions
Shared element transitions: maintaining object continuity between screens
Staggered animations: creating visual rhythm in list and grid appearances
lecture_notes.demonstration: Prototyping a shared element transition — a card expanding into a detail screen with the image and title maintaining continuity throughout the animation
setup_requirements: Principle or After Effects for complex animations (optional)

review.value: Ease-out (decelerating) for elements entering the screen, ease-in (accelerating) for elements leaving, ease-in-out for elements moving between positions — linear motion feels robotic
An element (image, card, icon) smoothly transforms between its position/size on one screen to its position/size on the next — maintains spatial continuity and reduces cognitive load

learning_objectives: Design gesture-based interactions that are discoverable and forgiving
Map gestures to actions following platform conventions
Handle gesture conflicts and provide alternatives for accessibility
readings: Touch Design for Mobile Interfaces by Steven Hoober, Ch. 2-5
Apple Human Interface Guidelines: Gestures section

lecture_notes.introduction: Touch gestures are powerful but invisible. Unlike buttons, there is no visual affordance telling users that swiping, pinching, or long-pressing will do something. Gesture design must balance power with discoverability.
key_concepts: Core gestures: tap, double-tap, long press, swipe, pinch, rotate, drag
Gesture discoverability: coaching, progressive disclosure, visual hints
Platform conventions: iOS vs. Android gesture expectations
Touch targets: minimum 44x44pt (Apple), 48x48dp (Android)
Gesture alternatives: every gesture must have a non-gesture fallback for accessibility
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a photo gallery with pinch-to-zoom, swipe-to-navigate, and double-tap-to-zoom — including accessibility alternatives for each gesture

review.value: Apple: 44x44 points, Android: 48x48dp, WCAG: 24x24 CSS pixels minimum — smaller targets cause tap errors and frustrate users, especially those with motor impairments
Unlike buttons, gestures have no visual affordance. Solutions: first-use coaching overlays, subtle visual hints (peeking content, drag handles), and always providing a visible button alternative

class_schedule.topic: Form Design and Data Entry Patterns
learning_objectives: Design forms that minimize user effort and error rates
Implement inline validation that guides rather than punishes
Choose appropriate input types and interaction patterns for different data types
readings: Form Design Patterns by Adam Silver, Ch. 1-5
Web Form Design by Luke Wroblewski, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Forms are where user experience often breaks down. Every extra field, confusing label, or harsh error message increases abandonment. Great form design minimizes friction while ensuring data quality.
key_concepts: One-column layouts outperform multi-column for most forms
Label placement: above-field labels for speed, inline labels for space
Inline validation timing: on blur (not on keystroke) for most fields
Smart defaults and pre-population to reduce user effort
Progressive disclosure: showing fields only when relevant
lecture_notes.demonstration: Redesigning a registration form — reducing fields, improving validation, adding smart defaults, and testing with screen readers
setup_requirements: Screen reader for accessibility testing (VoiceOver or NVDA)

review.value: Validate on blur (when user leaves the field), not on keystroke — keystroke validation shows errors while the user is still typing, which is both annoying and distracting
Show additional fields only when a previous selection makes them relevant — 'Do you have a promo code? [yes/no]' instead of always showing the promo code field

learning_objectives: Design error states that help users understand and recover from problems
Implement undo/redo patterns for destructive actions
Create graceful degradation experiences when systems partially fail
readings: Designing for Error by Jeff Johnson, Ch. 1-3
Defensive Design for the Web by 37signals, Ch. 3-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Errors are inevitable. The quality of a product is defined not by whether errors occur, but by how the product handles them. Good error design prevents, detects, and helps users recover from problems.
key_concepts: Error prevention: constraints, confirmations, and smart defaults
Error message anatomy: what happened, why, and how to fix it
Undo vs. confirmation dialogs: undo is almost always better
Graceful degradation: maintaining partial functionality when systems fail
Empty states as opportunities: guiding users toward first actions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing the error handling flow for a file upload feature — network failure, file too large, wrong format, server timeout — with recovery options for each scenario

review.value: Three parts: (1) What happened — 'Payment failed' (2) Why — 'Your card was declined' (3) How to fix it — 'Try a different card or contact your bank' — never show technical error codes to users
Undo ('Email archived. [Undo]') is better than confirmation ('Are you sure?') because undo does not interrupt the flow, reduces decision fatigue, and is forgiving rather than preventive

questions.question: Decompose a 'pull-to-refresh' interaction into its four microinteraction components: trigger, rules, feedback, and loops/modes.
questions.answer: Trigger: User pulls down from the top of a scrollable list past a threshold distance. Rules: If pull distance exceeds threshold, initiate data refresh from server; if released before threshold, snap back with no action. Feedback: A spinner or animated indicator appears at the top, the list content moves down to reveal it, and upon completion the spinner disappears and new content appears at the top. Loops: While refreshing, the spinner animates continuously; if refresh fails, show an error message with retry option. On repeated use, the animation remains consistent but a cooldown may prevent excessive server requests.

rubric.key: Trigger correctly identified with threshold concept
Rules include both success and below-threshold cases
Feedback describes visual, motion, and completion states
Loops address ongoing animation and repeated use

questions.question: Explain why linear easing (constant speed) feels unnatural in UI animations, and describe when you would use ease-out versus ease-in-out easing curves.
questions.answer: Linear easing feels unnatural because nothing in the physical world moves at constant speed — objects accelerate and decelerate due to friction, gravity, and inertia. UI elements should mimic physical behavior to feel intuitive. Use ease-out (starts fast, decelerates) for elements entering the screen — the element quickly arrives and gently settles into place, feeling responsive. Use ease-in-out (accelerates then decelerates) for elements moving between two positions on screen — the smooth start and stop feels like a natural repositioning. The general rule: ease-out for entrances, ease-in for exits, ease-in-out for movement within the view.

rubric.key: Explains why linear feels unnatural with physical world reference
Mentions general rule or additional easing context

questions.question: A mobile app uses a swipe-left gesture to delete items from a list with no confirmation and no undo. Identify the interaction design problems and propose a better design.
questions.answer: Problems: (1) Destructive action with no recovery path — accidental swipes permanently delete data. (2) No confirmation or undo means the consequence of a mistake is severe. (3) Swipe-left is an invisible gesture with no affordance — new users may not discover it, and accidental swipes are common during scrolling. Better design: (1) Add an undo snackbar that appears for 5-8 seconds after deletion ('Item deleted. [Undo]'), allowing immediate recovery. (2) Show a subtle swipe hint (peeking red background) to make the gesture discoverable. (3) Require the full swipe to complete the action (partial swipe shows 'Delete' button that requires a second tap). (4) Provide a visible delete button in an edit mode as a non-gesture alternative.

questions.question: Design the validation behavior for an email address field. Specify when validation occurs, what the error message says, and how the field recovers when the user corrects their input.
questions.answer: Validation behavior: (1) Validate on blur (when user tabs or clicks away from the field) — not on keystroke, which would show errors while typing. (2) Check for: empty field (if required), missing @ symbol, missing domain, invalid format. (3) Error message appears below the field in red: 'Enter a valid email address (e.g., name@example.com)' — shows the expected format. (4) The field border turns red and an error icon appears. (5) Recovery: as soon as the user returns to the field and begins typing, the error message changes to a neutral hint state. On the next blur, validation runs again. If now valid, the field returns to normal with a subtle green checkmark. (6) For real-time assistance: after the @ symbol, offer domain autocomplete suggestions (gmail.com, outlook.com).

rubric.key: Validates on blur, not on keystroke
Error message is specific and includes expected format
Explains recovery behavior when user corrects input

major_assignments.title: Interaction Design System
major_assignments.description: Design and document a comprehensive interaction design system for a mobile application. Create a library of reusable interaction patterns covering navigation, data entry, feedback, error handling, and motion — all prototyped and tested.
objectives: Design a cohesive set of interaction patterns for a mobile app
Prototype each pattern with appropriate motion and feedback
Document interaction specifications for developer handoff
Test key interactions with users and iterate based on findings
deliverables: Interaction pattern library: at least 12 patterns covering navigation, forms, feedback, gestures, and error handling
Interactive Figma prototype demonstrating all patterns in context
Interaction specification document with timing, easing, and behavior details
Usability test findings for 3 key interaction patterns
Motion design rationale document explaining each animation choice

criteria: Patterns cover all key interaction categories
Each pattern has clearly defined trigger, rules, feedback, and loops
Patterns are consistent with each other in style and behavior
Edge cases and error states are addressed for each pattern
All patterns are functional in the prototype
Motion design is purposeful and well-timed
Transitions feel smooth and natural
Prototype demonstrates patterns in realistic app context
Specifications are detailed enough for developer implementation
Timing, easing, and trigger conditions clearly defined
Motion rationale is grounded in interaction design principles
Documentation format is clear and professional
At least 3 patterns tested with real users
Test scenarios target specific interaction qualities
Findings clearly documented with iteration evidence
Final patterns reflect improvements from testing
Comprehensive library of well-designed interaction patterns
Polished prototype with purposeful motion design
Professional documentation suitable for developer handoff
Evidence-based iteration from user testing

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on interaction design principles, motion design, and pattern analysis

interaction_labs.description: Weekly labs designing and prototyping specific interaction patterns in Figma

interaction_design_system.description: Complete interaction pattern library with prototypes, documentation, and testing

interaction_critiques.description: Weekly analysis and critique of real-world interaction design examples

final_presentation.description: Presentation of interaction design system to a panel of design professionals

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for lab sessions and guest speaker presentations. More than 3 unexcused absences will result in a half-letter grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Interaction labs are due before the next lab session. Late labs lose 15% per day. The final project has a firm deadline with 10% per day penalty.
course_policies.collaboration: Interaction pattern research may be collaborative. All design work, prototypes, and documentation must be individual. Sharing Figma prototype files is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All interaction designs must be original. Reproducing existing patterns from design system libraries without significant adaptation and attribution is academic dishonesty.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Figma (with prototyping features), mobile device for prototype testing, screen recording software, and optionally Principle or After Effects for complex animations.
